title: Title states the message, not the topic
body: Write each slide title as a full-sentence assertion carrying the one point of the slide, not a topic label. Bad: 'FC-BGA Structure'. Good: 'Flip-chip bumps shorten the interconnect vs wire bond'. The body (figure/bullets) is the evidence for that assertion. One assertion per slide.

title: One idea per slide
body: Each slide carries exactly one idea. If a slide needs 'and' to describe its point, split it. A cross-section plus its 3 benefits is two slides: the figure slide (structure) and a content slide (implications).

title: Bullets: parallel, phrase-form, one line
body: Use 3-5 bullets, each a parallel grammatical phrase (all noun phrases or all verb phrases), ideally under ~10 words and one line. Drop articles where clear. No sub-bullets in a technical summary slide. If prose is needed it is not a bullet slide.

title: Cap bullet count; prefer a figure
body: More than 6 bullets = cognitive overload; split or promote to a figure. For structure/process/comparison, a figure (stack/flow/compare) beats bullets. Reserve bullets for implications, specs, and takeaways that resist diagramming.

title: No paragraphs on slides
body: Slides are not documents. Never place a paragraph. Convert sentences to a headline assertion plus terse evidence. Detail belongs in speaker notes, not the slide face.

title: Every slide answers 'so what'
body: Close reasoning with the implication. After a fact ('TGV enables 40 um pitch') add the consequence ('=> finer L/S than organic core'). Prefer an explicit takeaway line or caption over leaving the reader to infer.

title: Maximize data-ink; remove chartjunk
body: Every mark should carry meaning. Remove decorative gradients, drop shadows, redundant borders, gratuitous color. In a cross-section, ink = layers, joints, vias, labels; everything else is noise. Let the palette encode material, not decoration.

title: Build one clear visual hierarchy
body: Rank elements: title assertion first, key figure/number second, supporting detail last. Convey rank with size, weight, and position (top-left or center for the lead). Exactly one focal point per slide; if two things compete, demote one.

title: Color is for meaning, used sparingly
body: Limit to a small purposeful palette (3-5 roles). Use a single accent to direct attention to the one thing that matters; make everything else neutral. In this system, material roles already carry color meaning, so do not add extra emphasis colors that fight the palette.

title: Type: one sans family, readable sizes
body: One sans-serif family; express hierarchy by size/weight, not new fonts. Keep body text large enough to read across a room (titles largest, then subtitle, then labels/captions). Avoid tiny callout labels; if a label must be long, shorten the wording instead of shrinking type.

title: Protect whitespace
body: Leave meaningful empty space (roughly a fifth of the slide). Do not fill margins with logos, boxes, or extra text. Whitespace creates separation and emphasis; a crowded technical slide reads as noise even when every item is correct.

title: Dark text on light; squint test
body: Default to dark text on a light slide (this system's convention). Ensure label text over dark materials (mold, PCB, silicon) flips to light. Test: squinting from a distance, the title and the key label must still be legible.

title: Align everything to a grid
body: Consistent alignment separates professional from amateur slides. Titles, figures, captions, and callouts share margins and baselines. The layout engine owns geometry here; do not hand-place elements that break the shared grid.

title: Caption = scope + honesty
body: A figure caption states what the diagram is and its honest limits. Include 'not to scale' when thicknesses are exaggerated, and name the structure/variant. One short line. Do not restate the title; add the missing context.

title: Numbers: SI units, consistent precision
body: Use SI units with a space before the unit (100 um, 2.0 TB/s), consistent significant figures, and thousands separators for readability. Never invent numbers; if a figure is needed but unknown, mark it as data-needed rather than guessing.

title: Expand an acronym on first use
body: Spell out a term once, then use the acronym (Through-Glass Via (TGV), then TGV). Match the audience: expert decks can stay terse; mixed audiences need the expansion. Keep one canonical spelling per term across the deck.

title: Comparisons: same axis, side by side
body: To compare A vs B, hold structure constant and vary one thing. Use a two-panel compare figure with identical layer framing so the difference (e.g., organic core vs glass core) pops. State the dimension of comparison in the caption.

title: Process: 4-6 steps, left to right
body: Show a process as a short left-to-right flow of 4-6 labeled steps. More than 6 steps should be grouped or split. Each node is a terse verb phrase (Solder paste print, Reflow, AOI). The arrow carries sequence; do not label every edge unless it adds information.

title: Pick template by meaning, not by images on hand
body: Choose composition from the message: one diagram explained => image+text; two structures compared => two-images or compare; one striking figure => hero; sequence => flow. Let content dictate template; do not force text into a hero or split a single idea across a grid.

title: Compose uploaded images for harmony
body: When placing user images: give each a one-line caption in the deck's voice, order them by narrative (context -> detail, or A -> B), keep captions parallel, and size images consistently. Deduplicate near-identical uploads. Match generated captions to the terminology used elsewhere in the deck.

title: Signpost deck structure
body: A multi-slide deck reads as an arc: context -> structure -> implications -> takeaway. Section/title slides orient the audience. Ensure titles, read alone as a sentence outline, tell the whole story (the 'headline test').

title: Graphical integrity
body: Represent reality faithfully: relative layer thicknesses should be plausible even when exaggerated for visibility (say so), joints should match the real interconnect (barrel for C4, pillar for Cu-pillar bump), and comparisons must not distort scale to favor a conclusion.

title: Label what matters; avoid clutter
body: Label the features the assertion depends on, not every layer. Prefer inline labels for wide bands and callouts for thin ones. Keep callout wording short so leader lines stay uncrossed and readable. If a stack has many thin films, label groups, not each film.

title: Title length fits one or two lines
body: An assertion title should fit one to two lines at heading size. If it runs longer, tighten the claim; move nuance to the caption or notes. A crisp claim is more memorable than a hedged one.

title: Match depth to audience
body: Executive audiences want the implication and one number; engineers want the mechanism and structure. Keep the same facts but shift emphasis: lead executives with the so-what, lead engineers with the how. Never pad an expert deck with definitions they know.
